findings.message: Logging the caller's email address (PII) at INFO level violates GDPR data minimisation and purpose-limitation principles. Application logs typically have broad access and long retention without a defined erasure path, which makes right-to-erasure (Art. 17) impractical and creates an unbounded PII store.
findings.suggestion: Do not log the raw email. Either drop the identifier entirely, pseudonymise it (e.g. salted HMAC/hash), or replace it with an opaque internal user id. If audit is required, write to a dedicated audit log with a documented retention period and deletion hooks tied to erasure requests.

findings.message: Accepting the user's email as a query parameter (and logging it) means PII flows through URLs/access logs/proxies with no retention or deletion policy, and no documented lawful basis or purpose. This is a personalisation feature with no consent, retention schedule, or erasure path.
findings.suggestion: Derive the user identity from the authenticated session/JWT instead of a query string, document a retention period for any stored personalisation data, and define an erasure path that purges the user's personalisation footprint on Art. 17 request.

findings.message: Sensitive operation (per-user personalised search by email) has no audit log — only an informal INFO log that mixes PII into the application log. There is no tamper-evident, retention-bounded audit trail recording who searched what and when.
findings.suggestion: Emit a structured audit event (separate sink from app logs) with a pseudonymous actor id, action, timestamp, and correlation id; define retention and access controls; keep PII out of the audit payload.
